title: std::unique_ptr

exercises.title: Manage a single resource with unique_ptr
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Write a program that creates a `Logger` object via `std::unique_ptr`, uses it, then transfers ownership to a second pointer and verifies the original is empty.

## Input

- A single line containing one integer `id` (1 <= id <= 9999) representing the logger ID.

## Output

Print exactly five lines in this order:
1. `Logger <id> created`
2. `Logger <id> logging`
3. `Transferred: original is null = true`
4. `New owner has logger <id>`
5. `Logger <id> destroyed`

Replace `<id>` with the integer read from stdin.

## Requirements

- Define `struct Logger` with an `int id` member, a constructor that prints `Logger <id> created`, a member function `void log() const` that prints `Logger <id> logging`, and a destructor that prints `Logger <id> destroyed`.
- In `main`, create the logger using `std::make_unique<Logger>(id)`. Do not use `new` or `delete`.
- Call `log()` on the unique_ptr (use `->`).
- Transfer ownership to a second `unique_ptr` using `std::move`.
- Print line 3 using `std::boolalpha` and checking whether the original pointer equals `nullptr`.
- Print line 4 by accessing `id` through the new owner.
- The destructor message (line 5) must appear last when the owning pointer goes out of scope.

test_cases.label: sample -- id 10
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Logger 10 created
Logger 10 logging
Transferred: original is null = true
New owner has logger 10
Logger 10 destroyed

test_cases.label: hidden -- id 1 (minimum)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Logger 1 created
Logger 1 logging
Transferred: original is null = true
New owner has logger 1
Logger 1 destroyed

test_cases.label: hidden -- id 9999 (maximum)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Logger 9999 created
Logger 9999 logging
Transferred: original is null = true
New owner has logger 9999
Logger 9999 destroyed

exercises.title: Build and transfer a unique_ptr collection
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Write a program that creates multiple `Item` objects managed by `std::unique_ptr`, stores them in a `std::vector`, and then moves one item out of the vector to demonstrate ownership transfer with containers.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 10) representing the number of items to create.
- Line 2: an integer `idx` (0 <= idx < n) representing the index of the item to extract from the vector.

## Output

1. For each item created (in order from 1 to n): `Item <i> created`
2. `Vector size: <n>`
3. `Extracted item <id>`
4. `Vector size after extract: <n>` (size stays the same -- the slot becomes nullptr)
5. Destruction messages as items go out of scope. The extracted item is destroyed first (end of main), then the vector's remaining items are destroyed in order of their index position. Items whose slot is nullptr produce no output.

## Requirements

- Define `struct Item` with an `int id` member, a constructor that prints `Item <id> created`, and a destructor that prints `Item <id> destroyed`.
- In `main`, read `n` and `idx`. Create a `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Item>>` and use a loop to `push_back` items with ids from 1 to n (use `std::make_unique`).
- Print the vector size.
- Move the element at index `idx` out of the vector into a local `unique_ptr` using `std::move`. This leaves `nullptr` in the vector slot.
- Print the extracted item's id and the vector size after extraction.
- Do not use `new`, `delete`, or `erase`. The vector size must remain `n` after extraction.

test_cases.label: sample -- 3 items, extract index 1
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Item 1 created
Item 2 created
Item 3 created
Vector size: 3
Extracted item 2
Vector size after extract: 3
Item 2 destroyed
Item 1 destroyed
Item 3 destroyed

test_cases.label: hidden -- 1 item, extract index 0
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Item 1 created
Vector size: 1
Extracted item 1
Vector size after extract: 1
Item 1 destroyed

test_cases.label: hidden -- 5 items, extract index 4
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Item 1 created
Item 2 created
Item 3 created
Item 4 created
Item 5 created
Vector size: 5
Extracted item 5
Vector size after extract: 5
Item 5 destroyed
Item 1 destroyed
Item 2 destroyed
Item 3 destroyed
Item 4 destroyed
